insights: All five managerial assignees handle exactly 100 tickets each, indicating zero variance in workload distribution and eliminating evidence of individual management overload.
All five assignment groups are assigned exactly 100 tickets each, confirming structural balance across team-level administrative responsibility and ruling out group-level resource concentration.
Administrative actions are nearly equally distributed among 'admin' (167), 'employee' (167), and 'system' (166), showing no overreliance on managerial personnel for system user administration tasks.

The absence of missing values across all 14 columns confirms data integrity and strengthens confidence in the conclusion that no hidden imbalances or unrecorded overload patterns exist in this dataset.

No statistical or distributional anomalies were detected across any dimension relevant to managerial role distribution—including location, category, and priority—further supporting the absence of systemic imbalance in IT administrative workload.
summary: The analysis directly addresses the goal of identifying management overload risks in IT system user administration and finds no evidence of imbalance across any evaluated dimension. Managerial assignees, assignment groups, and administrative action sources all exhibit perfect or near-perfect uniformity in ticket distribution, indicating equitable workload allocation and no concentration of responsibility that would suggest overload. Data completeness further validates this null finding as robust rather than artifact-driven. Given these results, management focus should shift from rebalancing assignments to validating whether current workload levels themselves are sustainable—or whether upstream demand drivers (e.g., user provisioning volume, policy changes) warrant proactive capacity planning.
